configure-and-initialize-a-repository.label: Initialize Your First Repository
configure-and-initialize-a-repository.summary: Turn an ordinary project folder into a Git repository on main, inspect the metadata Git created, and set a repository-only author identity.

sketch3.title: Ask Git for the First Status

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Initialize an Ordinary Folder
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `trail-journal/` repository.

The `trail-journal/` folder already contains a README but has no history. initialize Git with `main` as the first branch, and print the current branch.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: git init creates repository metadata without changing the project files already in the folder.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.starterCode:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `trail-journal/` repository.

The `trail-journal/` folder already contains a README but has no history. initialize Git with `main` as the first branch, and print the current branch.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

main_sh.content:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf trail-journal
mkdir -p trail-journal
cd trail-journal
printf %s '# Trail Journal
' > README.md
cd ..

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf trail-journal
mkdir -p trail-journal
cd trail-journal
printf %s '# Trail Journal
' > README.md
cd ..

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Read What Git Created
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `trail-journal/` repository.

`trail-journal/` is now a repository. List its `.git` directory, then read `.git/HEAD` to confirm that HEAD points to `main`.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: The .git directory stores repository metadata, and HEAD identifies the currently checked-out branch reference.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.starterCode:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `trail-journal/` repository.

`trail-journal/` is now a repository. List its `.git` directory, then read `.git/HEAD` to confirm that HEAD points to `main`.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

main_sh.content:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf trail-journal
mkdir -p trail-journal
cd trail-journal
printf %s '# Trail Journal
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C trail-journal init -q -b main

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf trail-journal
mkdir -p trail-journal
cd trail-journal
printf %s '# Trail Journal
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C trail-journal init -q -b main

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Set a Repository-Only Identity
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `trail-journal/` repository.

Set the author name to `ZoeSkoul Learner` and the email to `learner@zoeskoul.local` inside `trail-journal/` only. Print both local values afterward.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: Repository-local identity applies only to this project and does not alter the machine-wide Git configuration.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.starterCode:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.solutionCode:
git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
git config --local --get user.name
git config --local --get user.email

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Ask Git What It Sees
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `trail-journal/` repository.

The repository and local identity are ready. Use short status to see the untracked `README.md` before staging anything.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: git status --short summarizes the relationship between the working tree and the staging area.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.starterCode:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.instructions:

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

main_sh.content:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf trail-journal
mkdir -p trail-journal
cd trail-journal
printf %s '# Trail Journal
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C trail-journal init -q -b main
git -C trail-journal config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C trail-journal config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of Version Control
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of version control systems like Git?
single-choice-1.hint: Think about how version control helps manage changes over time.

help.concept: Version control systems help manage changes to files and coordinate work on those files among multiple people.
help.hint_1: Version control allows you to track changes and collaborate with others.
help.hint_2: It helps prevent data loss and manage project history.

options.a: To track changes and collaborate on projects

single-choice-2.title: Repository Initialization
single-choice-2.prompt: What command is used to initialize a new Git repository?
single-choice-2.hint: This command sets up the .git directory.

help.concept: Initializing a repository is the first step in using Git for version control.
help.hint_1: The command is short and starts with 'git'.
help.hint_2: It creates the necessary metadata for version control.

multi-choice-1.title: Components of a Git Repository
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which of the following are components of a Git repository? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Think about the parts that make up the repository's structure.

help.concept: A Git repository consists of several components that manage the project's history and configuration.
help.hint_1: Consider what stores the history and configuration of the repository.
help.hint_2: These components are essential for tracking changes and managing the repository.

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of Git Setup Steps
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps to set up a new Git repository in the correct order.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with configuring the identity and end with checking the status.

help.concept: Setting up a Git repository involves several key steps to ensure it's ready for version control.
help.hint_1: Begin with identity configuration and end with verifying the setup.
help.hint_2: Think about the logical sequence of preparing a repository.

fill-blank-choice-1.title: Git Identity Configuration
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Complete the command to set the local Git user name: `git config --local user.name [blank1]`
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: The name should be enclosed in quotes.

help.concept: Setting the local user name is crucial for identifying the author of commits.
help.hint_1: The name should match the format used in the example.
help.hint_2: Ensure the name is enclosed in quotes.

fill-blank-choice-2.title: Git Email Configuration
fill-blank-choice-2.prompt: Complete the command to set the local Git user email: `git config --local user.email [blank1]`
fill-blank-choice-2.hint: The email should be enclosed in quotes.

help.concept: Setting the local user email is crucial for identifying the author of commits.
help.hint_1: The email should match the format used in the example.
help.hint_2: Ensure the email is enclosed in quotes.

sketch0.title: Create the Repository
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
A Git project begins as an ordinary folder. Nothing is tracked until you initialize the folder as a repository. Enter the project directory first, then run:

```bash
git init -b main
```

The command creates Git’s private metadata inside the current folder and names the first branch `main`. It does not upload anything, change your project files, or create a commit.

Read the command from left to right: `git init` creates the repository; `-b main` chooses the initial branch name. The location matters. Running it one directory too high would make the wrong folder the repository.

sketch1.title: What Git Added
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Initialization adds a hidden `.git` directory. Your visible project files remain where they were, while `.git` stores repository configuration, references, and future commit objects.

You can confirm that Git recognizes the folder without editing its internals:

```bash
git rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree
git branch --show-current
```

The first command prints `true`; the second prints `main`. Treat `.git` as Git-managed data. Looking is safe, but manually changing files inside it can damage the repository.

sketch2.title: Ask Git for the First Status
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
A new repository usually contains project files that Git has not started tracking. Ask Git to describe the situation:

```bash
git status --short
```

Suppose the folder contains `route-notes.txt`. Git prints:

```text
?? route-notes.txt
```

The two question marks mean the file is untracked. Git can see it in the working folder, but no snapshot contains it yet. Status is a reading command: it tells you what Git knows before you decide what to stage or commit.

sketch3.title: Set a Local Identity
sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
Commits record an author name and email. In a learning workspace, keep that identity inside the repository instead of changing the entire machine:

```bash
git config --local user.name "Avery Chen"
git config --local user.email "avery@example.test"
```

The first line stores the author name. The second stores the matching email. Because both commands use `--local`, the values apply only to the current repository.

Identity configuration does not create a commit. It prepares Git to attach clear authorship when you create one later.
